title: Envelope-back cushion cover
subtitle: Three rectangles, two overlapping back panels, no zip required
excerpt: The envelope-back cushion cover is the cushion cover for sewers who don't yet do zips. Three rectangles — one front, two backs that overlap — stitched together inside-out and turned through. The cushion pad slides through the overlap, which closes flat under the weight of the pad. Three sizes given (40, 45, 50 cm square) for the standard UK cushion pads.
sourceNotes: WWII Make Do and Mend pamphlet (UK Board of Trade, 1943), § Furnishing Repairs, pp 32-34, for the envelope-back construction — Crown Copyright expired. Beeton's Book of Needlework (1870), § Cushions, pp 78-82, Project Gutenberg etext #25640, for the proportions and the hem-the-back-edges-first construction sequence. Singer Sewing Library, 1950s, Internet Archive, for the modern overlap-width recommendation (12 cm overlap on a 45 cm cushion).

glossaryTerms.definition: The section where two back panels of an envelope cushion cover cross over each other in the middle of the cushion's back. The overlap closes flat under the weight of the cushion pad, so no zip or buttons are needed to keep the cover closed. The standard overlap is 12-15 cm on a 45 cm cushion.

Extra fabric beyond the bare minimum dimension, allowing for movement, fit, or — in cushion covers — the slight rounded thickness of the filled cushion pad. A cushion cover is cut tight to the pad (no positive ease) so the cover stretches over the pad firmly. A garment is cut with positive ease so it fits the body comfortably.

content.text: An envelope cushion cover is three rectangles. One is the front of the cushion; the other two are smaller pieces that form the back, overlapping each other in the middle. The pad slides into the cover through the overlap, and the weight of the pad inside holds the overlap flat. No zip, no buttons, no Velcro — just two hemmed panels that cross over.

This is the project for a new sewer who has finished a drawstring bag and an apron and is ready for the next step. It introduces accurate piece-cutting (three rectangles, two of them at different widths), hemming a long straight edge, and the construction order where pieces overlap before being stitched into a sandwich. Everything is rectangles and right-angled corners; no curves.
What you'll need
Materials (for a 45 cm cushion):
0.65 metres of medium- to heavy-weight cotton or linen — cotton drill, cotton canvas, medium linen, heavy linen, or a robust quilting cotton. Standard 112 cm fabric width.
All-purpose polyester thread, Tex 27 / size 50, in a colour matching the fabric.
One 45 cm square cushion pad. UK standard pads come in 40, 45, 50, and 55 cm squares; the dimensions table below covers 40, 45, and 50 cm.
Equipment:
Sewing machine with a size 90 universal needle.
Iron and pressing cloth.
Tape measure, dressmaking scissors or rotary cutter and mat, tailor's chalk, pins.
Before you start
Pre-wash and iron the fabric. Wash on the cycle the finished cover will live with — most cushion covers see a 40°C wash.
Identify the right side of the fabric. Mark it with a pin at one corner. Identify the warp direction (parallel to the selvedge).
All seams are 1 cm unless otherwise noted.
Cutting plan
The cushion cover is cut to the exact dimensions of the pad — no positive
ease

. A cushion cover cut larger than the pad ends up loose and saggy; cut tight to the pad, the cover stretches over and reads plump and well-filled. The 1 cm seam allowance is built into the dimensions below.
For a 45 cm pad — what this tutorial covers in detail:
Front: one rectangle 47 × 47 cm (i.e. 45 cm pad + 1 cm seam allowance on each side).
Back panel A (the lower overlap): one rectangle 47 × 32 cm.
Back panel B (the upper overlap): one rectangle 47 × 32 cm.
When the two back panels overlap by 12 cm in the centre of the cushion, they cover the same 47 cm height as the front (32 + 32 - 12 = 52, minus the two 2.5 cm hems on the overlapping edges = 47).
Other sizes:
40 cm pad: Front 42 × 42 cm; two back panels each 42 × 29 cm; overlap 10 cm.
50 cm pad: Front 52 × 52 cm; two back panels each 52 × 35 cm; overlap 14 cm.

Cut all three pieces with the longer dimension along the warp (parallel to selvedge). The grain matters — fabric distorts on the bias, and a cushion cover cut off-grain develops twisted edges after a few washes.
Construction
1. Hem the overlap edges of the back panels
Each back panel has one edge that will overlap the other panel in the centre of the cushion back. That edge needs a double hem so it sits neat and clean inside the finished cover.
Take back panel A. The 47 cm-long edge that will face the centre overlap is hemmed first. Fold and press 1 cm to the wrong side along this edge. Fold again by 1.5 cm and press. Pin.

Machine straight-stitch close to the inner folded edge — 2 mm in. Backstitch at both ends. The hem total is 2.5 cm; the visible side of the panel now has 2.5 cm of fabric folded under at the overlap edge.
Repeat for back panel B — same edge length, same double hem, same stitching.
Press both hems flat from both sides.
2. Lay out the cover for assembly
Lay the front piece on a flat surface, right side up.

Place back panel A on top of the front, wrong side up (so the right sides of the two pieces face each other). Align the top edge of panel A with the top edge of the front. The hemmed edge of panel A will run horizontally across the middle of the cover; the raw edges of panel A's three other sides align with the front's top, left, and right edges. Pin.

Now place back panel B on top, wrong side up, aligning the bottom edge of panel B with the bottom edge of the front. The hemmed edge of panel B runs horizontally across the middle too — and will overlap with panel A's hemmed edge by 12 cm. Pin.

Check: all four raw edges of the front piece should be matched to raw edges of one back panel or the other. The two back hems sit horizontally inside the cover, overlapping each other in the middle by 12 cm.
3. Stitch around the perimeter

Machine straight-stitch the entire perimeter of the cover at 1 cm seam allowance, with stitch length 2.5 mm. Backstitch at the start and end (which will be the same point if you stitch in a continuous loop).

Take care over the two points where the back panels' hemmed edges cross the side seams of the cover. The hem is several layers thick at that point — slow the machine down and walk the fabric through with the hand-wheel for the last few stitches before the corner. A skipped stitch at that crossing is the cushion-cover failure point.
4. Finish the seam allowances

On medium- to heavy-weight cottons and linens, the raw seam edges will fray inside the cover with use. Finish them: zigzag-stitch close to the raw edges around the entire perimeter (a basic zigzag at 3 mm width and 2 mm length works on every domestic machine). Pinking the edges with pinking shears is an acceptable alternative on lighter fabrics.

Clip the four corners diagonally to within 3 mm of the seam line. This reduces bulk at the corners and lets the cover sit square when turned right-side out. Don't clip into the seam — the 3 mm margin is the safety zone.
5. Turn through and press
Reach into the cover through the back
overlap
and turn the cover right side out. Push out each corner from the inside — a wooden chopstick or a knitting needle is the standard tool. Do not stab through the corner; nudge it firmly outward.

Press the finished cover flat — front and back. Use a pressing cloth on linen to prevent shine; cotton presses dry without a cloth at the wool setting. The corners should be sharp and square; the hemmed back edges should sit flat against each other in the middle of the back.
6. Insert the cushion pad

Pinch one corner of the pad and push it through the overlap into one corner of the cover. Work the pad into the corners of the cover — the cover is tight to the pad and takes some pulling. Smooth the pad flat inside the cover. The two back panels close over the pad and the weight of the pad inside holds them flat.
If the cover is hard to fit, it's correct — the no-ease cut is intentional. A loose cushion cover ages badly; the tight cut keeps the cushion looking full for the life of the pad.
Variations

Button-back: the upper panel's hemmed edge can carry two or three buttonholes, with corresponding buttons stitched onto the lower panel below the overlap. The buttons fasten the overlap closed and add a decorative detail. Button-and-buttonhole technique is a separate tutorial.

Piped edge: a length of piping (cord wrapped in a bias strip of fabric) stitched into the perimeter seam gives a tailored finish. Separate technique tutorial — piping is the next step after envelope cushions.
Two-fabric cover: cut the front from one fabric and the two back panels from another. Useful for using up scraps; visually striking when the two fabrics are coordinated rather than identical.
Bolster cover (cylindrical cushion): a separate construction — two rectangles wrapped around the bolster, gathered at each end. Separate tutorial.
Care

Remove the pad before washing; the pad rarely needs washing as often as the cover. Cotton and linen covers wash at 40°C; iron flat while slightly damp. The cushion pad lives 3-5 years on a well-used sofa; the cover lives many more if washed regularly.
Troubleshooter

attrs.heading: If something went sideways
attrs.intro: The common ways a first envelope-back cushion cover under-performs, and the fix.

items.symptom: Cover is loose around the pad after fitting
items.cause: Cut too generous — extra ease added beyond the seam allowance.
items.fix: Re-cut: front the exact pad size + 1 cm seam allowance on each side; no more. Tight is correct for cushion covers.

items.symptom: Corners of the cover read rounded, not square
items.cause: Corners not clipped before turning; or corner not pushed all the way out from inside.
items.fix: Clip the seam allowance diagonally at each corner to 3 mm before turning. Push each corner from inside with a chopstick or knitting needle.

items.symptom: Back overlap gaping open when the cushion is leaned against
items.cause: Overlap too small — under 10 cm on a 45 cm cushion.
items.fix: Re-cut the two back panels with 4 cm more height each, giving a 16 cm overlap. The wider overlap stays closed under load.

items.symptom: Seam coming open at the point where the two back hems cross the side seam
items.cause: Skipped or missed stitch at the thick crossing — the hem is several layers there.
items.fix: Re-stitch over that crossing, walking the machine slowly with the hand-wheel for the last 2-3 stitches before the corner. Use a size 100 jeans needle if working on heavy linen or canvas.

items.symptom: Cover twisting on the pad after a few uses
items.cause: Pieces cut off-grain — the front or back panels not aligned to the warp.
items.fix: Cut a new cover with the longer dimension of every piece running parallel to the selvedge. A grain-aligned cover stays square; an off-grain cover always twists.
